design_philosophy.core_loop: Eliminate opposing team members by throwing a volleyball at them while defending against incoming throws. Manage six supporting players and a captain with power shot abilities across multiple rounds.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Real-time ball possession and positioning: catching/intercepting vs. throwing/passing decisions, with team member elimination creating escalating pressure as lineup depletes.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a dodgeball team through a worldwide tournament, progressing from domestic rivals (Dallas vs. Chicago) to international opponents, culminating in championship victory.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Treats dodgeball as a legitimate competitive sport with roster management and tactical positioning. Contextualizes gameplay within Cold War geopolitics (USSR as final boss in US version) and cultural stereotyping of international teams. Emphasizes team composition over individual skill.

difficulty.lives_system: Team roster-based: seven members per team (captain + six players). Each character eliminated after 3-4 hits. Losing all members = match loss.
difficulty.checkpointing: Tournament progression: winning each match advances to next opponent. Winning cycle restarts after Japan stage.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Button configuration context switching provides tactical depth: left/right buttons change function based on ball possession (throw vs. pass; catch vs. duck). Dashing and power shots require deliberate input combinations. Difficulty appears to scale with opponent AI across tournament progression.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Tournament bracket advancement: win domestic match (Dallas vs. Chicago) → world cup stages (England, Iceland, China, Kenya/Africa, Japan) → cycle restart with England. NES version adds two additional teams (India, USSR) affecting stage order.
replayability_hooks: Bean Ball minigame (2-4 player party mode, supports up to 4 players in Japanese version)
Versus Play mode for head-to-head competition
Custom name entry system (Japanese version only) for team and player personalization
Multiple teams with distinct stats and formations encouraging different play strategies

world_building_techniques: Team nationalities and visual character designs (skin tones, hair colors changed for Western localization to reflect regional identity)
Stage-specific music reflecting cultural context (e.g., 'Sakura Sakura' for Japan stage, Beatles-inspired arrangement for England)
Tournament structure establishing stakes and progression narrative
Regional differences in team lineups and roster sizes implying cultural variation in dodgeball tradition
Trophy presentation by Superman parody character providing ceremonial climax

boss_design.philosophy: Tournament opponents function as escalating challenges with distinct team compositions and tactical formations. International teams employ larger rosters (more than four offensive players) with backup replacements, creating attrition-based difficulty curves.

faction_indicators: Team nationalities: USA (Dallas/Chicago), England, Iceland, China, Kenya, Japan, USSR (US version only)
Captain character designs and roster appearance reflecting national stereotypes
Team color palettes and formation strategies suggesting cultural identity
danger_signals: Cold War tension manifest in USSR as final boss in US release (late 1980s geopolitical context)
Caricatured character designs and cultural stereotyping in international team representations
Trophy awarded by Superman parody suggesting American exceptionalism in Western localization
environmental_storytelling: Stage backgrounds and court designs contextualizing each nation's cultural setting
Regional music themes establishing atmospheric identity for each tournament stage
Team roster sizes and backup player systems implying different competitive traditions across nations

sources_used: tcrf: Super Dodge Ball (NES) - The Cutting Room Floor
